does: This paper evaluates out-of-band defenses against prompt injection attacks in LLM agents—systems like CaMeL, FIDES, Progent, RTBAS, and FORGE that use capabilities-based controls, information-flow labels, and reference monitors rather than training-based defenses. The authors provide a structured framework organizing these defenses through classical security principles (Biba integrity, reference monitoring, least privilege) and conduct a critical meta-evaluation analyzing the limitations of static benchmark validation. They specify a formal threat model and adaptive evaluation protocol designed to test whether these defenses remain robust against defense-aware, adaptive attacks, mirroring prior work that revealed vulnerabilities in training-based prompt injection defenses when tested against adversarial attack strategies.
connects: This work builds on the established trajectory of prompt injection research in LLM agents, extending prior analysis of in-band (training-based) defenses that were broken by adaptive attacks. It responds to recent convergence on out-of-band defense mechanisms introduced around 2024–2026, which shifted from hoping models would learn to refuse malicious instructions toward enforcing security through external policy enforcement. The paper connects to classical computer security theory, particularly reference monitoring and integrity models from systems security literature, applying these frameworks to understand modern AI safety mechanisms. It also responds to methodological critiques in adversarial ML, where fixed benchmarks have repeatedly overestimated defense robustness until adaptive threat models expose weaknesses.
enables: This work establishes a rigorous evaluation methodology for testing LLM agent defenses against adaptive attackers, enabling future researchers to validate claims more robustly and discover vulnerabilities before deployment. By formalizing the threat model and adaptive evaluation protocol, it creates a standard for measuring defense effectiveness that goes beyond static benchmarks. The structured classification of defenses using classical security principles opens pathways for understanding which protection mechanisms handle specific classes of attacks and where coverage gaps exist. This framework could guide the design of hybrid or compositional defenses, spurring development of more fundamentally robust agent security architectures that account for adaptive adversaries from inception rather than discovering failures post-hoc.
